coredata.dc:title: Melamine sponge functionalized with carbon nanodots for the extraction of polyaromatic hydrocarbons and musks from environmental samples prior to their determination by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry
coredata.dc:creator: Veneti, Georgia
Chatzimitakos, Theodoros
Stalikas, Constantine
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the functionalization of melamine sponge (MeS) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and its use as a sorbent for the extraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and musks from environmental samples are presented. The MeS@GQDs is prepared by modification of the MeS with GQDs in a one-pot, single-step, in situ synthesis, using citric acid as carbon source. The produced MeS@GQDs was used to develop and optimize an analytical method based on solid-phase extraction mode, for the subsequent analysis of soil samples and treated effluents exploiting the extraction properties of the synthesized MeS@GQDs. The developed method not only presented satisfactory repeatability (RSD<8.7%) and inter-day repeatability (RSD<9.6%), but also its limits of quantification were found to be low (15-80 ng L−1). Moreover, the proposed method was linear in a wide range of concentrations (0.015-11.8 µg L−1) and the recoveries from difficult matrixes, such as treated effluents and soil, were satisfactory. Overall, the use of the sorbent for the development of a new analytical method was found to be advantageous as compared with other reported methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Melamine sponge
Graphene quantum dots
PAHs
Musks
GC-MS

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Functionalization of melamine sponges with GQDs (MeS@GQDs)
Choice and synthesis of the sorbent
Effect of the pH and temperature
Effect of organic matter and organic solvent
Reusability and selectivity of the sorbent

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of bare MeS (green spectrum), blank MeS (orange spectrum), MeS@GQDs (pink spectrum) and GQDs (blue spectrum).
(A) A water droplet on the surface of MeS@GQDs and the contact angle, (B) MeS and MeS@GQDs in a glass beaker containing water, (C) MeS@GQDs immersed in water.
SEM images of (A) bare MeS, (B) blank MeS and (C) MeS@GQDs.

Effect of the concentration of citric acid and the second modification οn the recovery percentage of PAHs and musks; n=3 (*two consecutive modifications of the sponge with a concentration of citric acid of 20 mg mL−1).
Effect of the pH on the recovery percentage of PAHs and musks.
Effect of different amounts of the two salts οn recovery percentage of PAHs and musks.
Influence of humic acid content on the recovery percentage of PAHs and musks.
Effect of the content of acetonitrile on the total extraction efficiency.
Effect of different solvents on the elution effectiveness.
Summary of analytical characteristics of the proposed method for determination of PAHs and musks.

row.entry: 0.4 µg L−1 PAHs 0.3 µg L−1 musks
0.8 µg L−1 PAHs 0.6 µg L−1 musks
0.4 µg L−1 PAHs 0.3 µg L−1 musks
0.8 µg L−1 PAHs 0.6 µg L−1 musks

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: RSD: relative standard deviation. Each experiment was performed in triplicate.

row.entry: MOF-MIL-101 (Cr) modified zero valent iron nanoparticles
QuEChERS/ dispersive solid-phase extraction and cleanup

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PDMS-DVB: polydimethylsiloxane-divinylbenzene, LVI: large volume injection, FLD: fluorescence detector,

head.ce:title: Melamine sponge functionalized with carbon nanodots for the extraction of polyaromatic hydrocarbons and musks from environmental samples prior to their determination by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 451 10 Ioannina, Greece
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 451 10 Ioannina, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the functionalization of melamine sponge (MeS) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and its use as a sorbent for the extraction of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and musks from environmental samples are presented. The MeS@GQDs is prepared by modification of the MeS with GQDs in a one-pot, single-step, in situ synthesis, using citric acid as carbon source. The produced MeS@GQDs was used to develop and optimize an analytical method based on solid-phase extraction mode, for the subsequent analysis of soil samples and treated effluents exploiting the extraction properties of the synthesized MeS@GQDs. The developed method not only presented satisfactory repeatability (RSD<8.7%) and inter-day repeatability (RSD<9.6%), but also its limits of quantification were found to be low (15-80 ng L−1). Moreover, the proposed method was linear in a wide range of concentrations (0.015-11.8 µg L−1) and the recoveries from difficult matrixes, such as treated effluents and soil, were satisfactory. Overall, the use of the sorbent for the development of a new analytical method was found to be advantageous as compared with other reported methods.

ce:section.ce:para: The occurrence and degree of distribution of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) in the environment has increased over the last several years as they are among the most common types of organic pollutants posing a serious threat to the health of humans and ecosystems [1]. PAHs are lipophilic chemical compounds consisting of two or more condensed aromatic rings and usually originate from anthropogenic activity such as incomplete combustion of organic matter or industrial pollution and enter the soil, river systems, drinking water, and groundwater, domestic sewers, and food. For the above reasons, the European Community and its Environmental Protection Agency suggest their inspection due to their high toxicity and carcinogenicity [2]. Apart from PAHs, other polycyclic compounds, such as polycyclic musks are commonly encountered in the environment due to their use in personal care products and perfumes [3]. Polycyclic musks are a group of synthetic musks widely used to improve product odors and have replaced naturally-produced musks because of their remarkably low cost [4]. After their use, they enter the environment almost unchanged. Galaxolide and celestolide, as representative members of this class of compounds, are mainly transported to the wastewater, but treatment plants are incapable of removing them completely. The total concentration of musks in surface waters ranges from 0.1 µg L−1 to some µg L−1 [5,6]. Their occurrence in the aquatic environment is an emerging issue in ecotoxicology, as they tend to bioaccumulate in aquatic organisms due to their lipophilic nature [3].

Because of the complexity of environmental matrices, sample pre-treatment is required to separate the target analytes from the sample substrate. Solid-phase extraction (SPE) is one of the most frequently used extraction methods among the sample pretreatment techniques [7]. The sorbing material of SPE column determines the affinity, selectivity, capacity of extraction for analytes οf interest, and efficiency, thus, making their choice a key element of the technique [8].

The melamine sponge (MeS) has sparked interest as an SPE material because of its striking features. Succinctly, MeS is a low-cost, low-density, high-porosity (> 99%) foam-like material with an open hole structure, high suction capacity, good elasticity, high thermal and mechanical stability, and chemical conversion ability. MeS consists of a formaldehyde-melamine-sodium bisulfite copolymer [9] and displays high sorption capacity for water due to its hydrophilic groups, such as hydroxyl, aldehyde groups, and ether bonds, introduced during synthesis [10]. Also, due to the presence of functional groups, the MeS can be modified to exhibit a superhydrophobic behavior [11]. These modifications have been utilized for oil-water separation applications [10,12] and also for analytical purposes, inter alia, for the microextraction of sulfonamides from food and environmental matrices [11,13].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a relatively new category of carbon-based nanomaterials with spherical shape and size less than 10 nm [14]. They combine the electrical properties deriving from sp2 carbon with distinctive optical properties, making them similar to the classic quantum semiconductor dots [15]. The very good dispersion of most of them in aqueous solutions and the feasibility of modification with other compounds are the result of the significantly large number of oxygen moieties that are embedded on the surface of GQDs. The physicochemical properties of GQDs, and therefore, their applicability depends on the precursor materials used and the method of synthesis [16]. In most cases, GQDs are used for bioimaging purposes and as fluorescent probes for the detection of various metals [17–23]. The active groups of GQDs (such as -COOH, -NH2, -OH, etc.) improve their dispersibility and hydrophilicity which makes them suitable for sample preparation [24]. Muckoya et al. prepared carbon nanodots from oats as an SPE material for the preconcentration and extraction of personal care products and organophosphorus pesticides in environmental matrices [25]. Another interesting example was the composite molybdenum disulfide/carbon dots for the determination of three brominated flame retardants from water samples using dispersive solid-phase extraction [26]. However, the applications of GQDs for sample preparation that reap their benefits are currently limited.

The purpose of this study is to develop a novel sorbent for the extraction of musks and PAHs from environmental samples and their determination by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry. The new material is based on the extraction properties of a MeS, which has been decorated with GQDs, in a one-pot, single-step, in situ synthesis after heating/carbonization of citric acid. The as-prepared sorbent was used in a solid-phase extraction mode to develop an optimized analytical method for the analysis of complex matrixes, such as soil and treated effluents.

Citric acid (≥ 99.5%) and all solvents used were at least of analytical grade and purchased from Sigma – Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). NaOH (>99%), HCl (37% w/w), and humic acids were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). For all experiments, double distilled water (DDW) was used. The PAHs (acenaphthylene, acenaphthene, fluorene, phenanthrene, anthracene, fluoranthene, pyrene, benzo[a]anthracene, indeno[1,2,3-cd]pyrene, dibenzo[a,h]anthracene, benzo[g,h,i]perylene) were obtained from Supelco. A mix containing 11 representative PAHs, each at a concentration of 10 µg mL−1 in acetonitrile was prepared. Stock standard solutions of each musk (celestolide, phantolide, galaxolide) were purchased from LGC Promochem (Wesel, Germany) and were properly diluted with acetonitrile, at a final concentration of 50 µg mL−1 (Tables S1 and S2 of Supplementary material). Melamine sponges were bought from a local market in Ioannina (Greece). A mix of 27 pesticides belonging to different classes was prepared at a concentration of 1000 µg mL−1, in acetonitrile and stored in an amber screw-capped vial, at −18 °C (Tables S3 and S4 of Supplementary material).

A Shimadzu GC-17A gas chromatograph (Kyoto, Japan) interfaced with a Shimadzu QP 5000 mass spectrometer and equipped with a split/splitless injector and an SLB-5-MS column (30 m × 0.25 mm i.d., 0.25 µm film thickness, Agilent J&W, Agilent Technologies, CA, USA) was used for the chromatographic separation and quantitation of the target compounds. Injections were performed using a beveled, needle tip (0.6 mm glass barrel i.d.; 0.11 mm needle i.d.) purchased from Hamilton (Reno, Nevada, USA). The carrier gas used was helium (purity ≥99.999%) at a flow rate of 1.0 ml min−1. The injection volume was 1.0 µL and the splitless mode was employed for 1.0 min. The solvent cut-time was set to 5 min. The temperatures of the injector and mass transfer line were set at 250 °C and 290 °C, respectively. The temperature of the column was programmed at 60 °C for 1 min, increased to 290 °C, at a rate of 8 °C/min and held for 9.5 min. The total run time was 39.25 min. The mass detector was operated in the electron impact mode at 70 eV and the electron multiplier voltage was set to 1.2 kV. The quantification of the analytes was performed in the selective ion monitoring (SIM) mode and the respective quantifier and qualifier ions are given in Table S1 of Supplementary material. A representative chromatogram is given in Fig. S1 of Supplementary material.

For the separation of pesticides, the solvent cut-time was set to 5 min and the temperatures of the injector and mass transfer-line were set at 250 °C and 290 °C, respectively. The temperature of the column was set at 50 °C for 1 min, increased to 150 °C, at a rate of 15 °C/min, and then increased to 260 °C at a rate of 4 °C/min and held for 10 min. The total run time was 45.17 min. The pesticide ions for SIM mode are shown in Table S4 of Supplementary material.

A Spectrum Two FTIR was used to record the FTIR spectra using an attenuated weakened total reflectance accessory (PerkinElmer, Cambridge, MA, USA). The approximate measurement of the contact angle of the water droplet on the surface of the sponge was performed using the Krita software (Krita Foundation, KDE, Deventer, the Netherlands). The SEM images were acquired using a JSM 5600 system from Jeol (Nieuw-Vennep, Netherlands), operating at 20 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of melamine sponges with GQDs (MeS@GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Melamine sponges were cut into pieces of cylindrical shape with a diameter of 1.5 cm and a length of 2.5 cm (henceforth called sponges, in contrast to the half sponges produced by cutting the former in half), washed with DDW and ethanol and left to dry. Subsequently, each cylindrical piece was soaked in 5 mL of an ethanolic solution containing 0.1 g of citric acid (i.e., 20 mg mL−1), and dried. The soaking / drying step was repeated until all the solution was soaked up. Then, the cylindrical sponges were transferred to an oven at 280 ℃, for 3 h.

Before extraction, the hydrophobic cylindrical MeS@GQDs was preconditioned, as follows: The sponge was placed in a glass syringe barrel and 5 mL of n-hexane were percolated through it to remove loosely attached GQDs and any nonpolar contaminants, followed by 10 mL of acetonitrile. Then, the organic solvents were washed off with 20 mL of DDW and the MeS@GQDs was transferred to a screw-capped bottle containing 50 mL of sample. After stirring for 30 min at 750 rpm, the MeS@GQDs was removed and placed in a glass syringe barrel, which was connected to a vacuum pump to remove the residual water. The elution was carried out by passing successively 4 mL of acetonitrile and 4 mL of ethyl acetate, each of them representing twice the hold-up volume of the sponge. Finally, the eluent was evaporated to 100 µL under a gentle nitrogen stream and was injected into the GC / MS system.

Wastewater was collected from the outlet of the municipal wastewater treatment plant of Ioannina (Greece). The sample was filtered through a paper filter to remove suspended particles and was stored at 4 ℃, until extraction.

A silty soil sample was collected from a remote area in Ioannina and was left at room temperature overnight to dry, prior to grinding and sieving. In a falcon tube, 2 g of the soil was mixed with 5 mL of acetonitrile and the mixture was vortexed for 2 min, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm, for 10 min. The procedure of solid-liquid extraction was repeated twice by adding 2×3 mL of acetonitrile. The supernatants were combined, spiked with a standard solution of PAHs and musks and after vortexing, they were evaporated to a final volume of 2.5 mL, under a gentle nitrogen stream. Finally, the solution (method blank, sample, or spiked sample) was made up to a final volume of 50 mL with DDW and the PAHs and musks were extracted, using the procedure described above.

To study the influence of the experimental parameters on the response-total area of the analytes (% extraction efficiency), a central composite design (CCD) was applied including four variables: sample volume (22-177 ml), number of half sponges (1-5), stirring rate (177-873 rpm) and stirring time (9-71 min). The applied CCD consisted of 27 experiments (N = 2k + 2k + C0 = 24 + 2*4 + 3 = 27 runs, where N is the total number of experiments required, k is the number of variables, 2k is axial runs and C0 is center point's runs) including 3 center points. The chemometric study for the multivariate optimization was carried out using the Design-Expert® Version 10 Software Trial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Choice and synthesis of the sorbent
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we synthesized GQDs on the surface of melamine sponge from citric acid, in a one-pot, single-step procedure, based on a previous study, where GQDs were prepared by heating citric acid (the carbon source) at various time intervals, from 30 min to 2 h [27]. In that study, it was seen that the longer the heating the more hydrophobic the produced GQDs due to the increased degree of carbonization. In our study, the interconnected 3D network of the sponge was modified by soaking it with an ethanolic solution of citric acid and heating at 280℃, for 3 h. In this way, the white color of the sponge turned brown. A bare melamine sponge was prepared following the same procedure except that pure ethanol instead of ethanolic citric acid was used. In this case, the color of blank MeS changed from white to yellow. The brown color of the modified melamine sponge indicates the formation of GQDs, which was further corroborated by the characteristic blue fluorescence of GQDs. Based on the previous study, the GQDs, under these conditions, are rendered hydrophobicand citric acid is converted into a graphite structure so that the functionalized sponge can interact with molecules through hydrophobic and π interactions [27].

Fig. 1 shows the FT-IR spectra of GQDs, MeS@GQDs, bare MeS, and heated MeS (soaked only in ethanol solution and heated). The broad bands in the range 3320 -3344 cm−1 common to the four spectra are due to –OH and –NH stretching vibrations of MeS [28]. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs shows two absorption bands at 1540 and 1323 cm−1, which are assigned to –C=O and O–H stretching vibrations, respectively. The peaks around 1545 and 1335 cm−1 in the three MeS, originate from –C=N but in the case of MeS@GQDs can, also, originate from stretching vibrations of bonds –C=O and O–H of the GQDs. The signal at around 1453 cm−1 ascribed to the –C–H stretching is visible in all samples. The bare MeS shows a peak at around 1160 cm−1 which corresponds to C–N amine stretching. This peak shifts and broadens in the spectrum of the heated MeS, which is a typical case when the molecules undergo significant H-bonding [28]. The same peak shows greater broadening in the spectra of MeS@GQDs and GQDs and is related to different stretching vibrations of –C–O–C– and –C–O– groups present in the GQDs [29]. The peak at 980 cm−1 in the spectrum of the bare MeS, which is ascribed to triazine ring bending does not appear in the other spectra, signifying that during thermal treatment the structure of triazine is destroyed [30]. Further verification for the synthesis of GQDs on the sponge and not of graphene oxide was presented by examining the material under a UV lamp bearing in mind that fluorescence is a unique characteristic of GQDs. After mechanical stress of the modified sponge and treatment with organic solvents, blue fluorescence was observed under a UV lamp.

After modification of MeS, the GQDs load on the surface of MeS was determined by weighing the sponges before and after modification. However, during carbonation, the weight of MeS decreased due to dehydration. To offset this effect, a batch of blank MeS was weighed to calculate the reduction percentage, which was estimated to be about 15%. Considering it, the results showed that the loading of GQDs was 0.22 g/g of sponge.

The bare MeS was white and highly hydrophilic; after modification with GQDs, it turned brown and highly hydrophobic. Although citric acid is water-soluble with plenty of carboxylic groups, the produced GQDs become hydrophobic due to the conversion of hydroxyl groups to C–O–C in the MeS@GQDs [27]. The hydrophobicity of the MeS@GQDs was proven by the spherical shape of a drop of water on the surface of MeS@GQDs with the contact angle being around 124o (Fig. 2 A) [31]. In addition, while the bare MeS sinks easily in a glass beaker filled with water, the MeS@GQDs floats on the surface of the water (Fig. 2B). When the MeS@GQDs is submerged in the water exerting external pressure, the surface of the MeS@GQDs appears like a silver-like mirror covered by air bubbles (Fig. 2C) [13,32].

Fig. 3 shows the SEM images of the bare MeS, blank MeS, and MeS@GQDs. It can be noted that the bare MeS is comprised of a 3D interconnected network with a diameter of the melamine skeleton being about 8.8 µm. The porous framework seems to be retained in the blank MeS and MeS@GQDs. This is important for maintaining the increased surface of the sorbent.

The synthesis of functionalized MeS was optimized to achieve the maximum sorption of the analytes. The assessment of the extraction efficiency of the resulting sponges was carried out in an aqueous sample (15 mL), at concentrations of 2.6 µg L−1 and 10 µg L−1 for PAHs and musks, respectively. Acetonitrile and ethyl acetate were used as elution solvents. The total extraction efficiency i.e., the amounts of analytes extracted in relation to their total amount, was the criterion used for the selection of the optimum parameters. The parameters of the synthesis studied were: temperature and time of heating, the concentration of citric acid, and the number of functionalization cycles.

The heating temperature was studied between 200 and 280℃, for 3 and 5 h. The melting point of anhydrous citric acid is 153℃ and according to a previous study, GQDs are prepared by pyrolyzing citric acid at 200℃ [27]. The heating temperature studied here was not higher than 280℃ to avoid burning the sponge. The results showed that as the temperature of the oven raised, the produced MeS@GQDs performed better considering that the extraction efficiency at 280℃ was 16.7% higher than that prepared at 200℃ (n=3, data not shown). This can be explained by the fact that polymeric blocks and carbon cores are formed via dehydration of the initial molecules, as the temperature of the reaction increases [28,33]. As a result, the optimal temperature was found to be 280℃. The heating time was also examined and it was found that the performance of resulting MeS@GQDs did not exhibit any statistically significant difference (p>0.05, n=3, data not shown), at 3 and 5 h. It is noteworthy that at lower temperatures and/or shorter heating time periods the produced MeS@GQDs have a light brown color, which fades away during the process of preconditioning of sponge with the organic solvents and the sponge regains its white color [13].

As citric acid is the only source of GQDs, its concentration can affect the percentage of analytes that could be extracted from the composite material. An unmodified sponge (blank MeS) was tested to determine whether the modification of the sponge influences the extraction of the analytes. The results indicated that the extraction efficiency of the blank sponge was, in total, 50% lower than that of the functionalized sponge. Four solutions containing 4, 12, 20, and 40 mg mL−1 of citric acid, in 5 mL of ethanol were prepared to functionalize a bare melamine sponge. Maximum recovery was attained at a concentration of 20 mg mL−1, as portrayed in Fig. 2. The highest concentration (40 mg mL−1) caused a decrease in the extraction efficiency of the sponge. This could be explained by the fact that as the concentration of citric acid increases, GQDs cannot be retained effectively on the surface of the sponge which becomessaturated. It is worth noting that this same pattern was observed for all analytes.

The synthesized MeS@GQDs were subjected to a second functionalization cycle to further enhance the adsorption properties. The results indicated a decrease in the extraction efficiency of sponges, similar to that observed at a citric acid concentration of 40 mg mL−1 (Fig. 4 ), corroborating the saturation of the sponge surface with GQDs.

A one-variable-at-a-time optimization procedure for the temperature, pH, ionic strength, organic matter, and the presence of organic solvent was performed. Other variables and interactions affecting the results have been identified by preliminary experiments and a separate design of experiment was applied to determine the combination of variable levels that will provide the optimum response. Hence, the sample volume, number of half sponges, stirring rate and the stirring time were considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the pH and temperature
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments were carried out in triplicate (n=3), in the pH range of 3.0 – 10.0. Rather expectedly, the results indicated that pH did not affect the extraction efficiency (Fig. 5 ) as the analytes of interest are not ionizable [34]. As a result, adsorption between the sorbent (MeS@GQDs) and the target organic compounds is probably due to hydrophobic interactions [35]. Moreover, π interactions can be developed between the GQDs of the MeS@GQDs and the target analytes, which have a multitude of aromatic rings in their structures. Therefore, the next experiments were performed without prior pH adjustment.

With respect to the temperature, an 18% reduction was noticed as the temperature increased from 20 ℃ to 35 ℃ (n=3, data not shown). The yield of sorption of the analytes was suppressed by increasing the temperature, signifying that the interaction is an exothermic process and some of the target analytes are volatile compounds [34]. Therefore, all extractions were carried out at room temperature.

The addition of a salt can either improve the extraction yield due to the salting-out effect or reduce it by reducing the diffusion coefficient due to the increase of the viscosity of the solution [36]. To examine which of the above effect would prevail in our case, NaCl and Na2SO4 at concentrations of 10 to 30% (w/v) were used. As can be seen in Fig. 6 (experiments were carried out in triplicate), the addition of 10% (w/v) salt reduces the extraction efficiency by over 50%, in both cases; the reduction was even greater at higher concentrations. Also, lower concentrations of Na2SO4 (0.5% and 1.5% w/v) showed that the recovery was lower than that in the absence of salt. The decrease in the recovery in the presence of either NaCl or Na2SO4 is probably due to the increase in the viscosity of the aqueous solution (the larger the electrostatic attraction between the water layers, the higher the viscosity of the solution) [37]. Increasing the viscosity of the solution delays the percolation through the MeS@GQDs 3D network, resulting in low mass transfer and reduced extraction efficiency [13].

The optimization of the four variables (sample volume, sorbent mass, stirring rate, and time) was performed by employing a multivariate experimental design. According to the results of % removal efficiency values of the applied CCD, only few statistically significant interactions were observed between the variables (A-B and B-D, Tables S5 and S6 of Supplementary material). As high stirring time makes the procedure cumbersome and tedious and high stirring rate may cause detachment of the sorbing phase from the sponge, a relatively low volume accompanied by a low stirring time was adopted. The optimal conditions of the four variables are the following: sample volume: 50 mL, number of sponges (0.05 g): 1, stirring rate: 750 rpm, and stirring time: 30 min.

High volume assumes high stirring rate and time for the maximum extraction efficiency to be achieved. Detailed data and discussion about the multivariate experiment are presented in Section S4 of the Supplementary Material (including Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of organic matter and organic solvent
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal working conditions, the impact of organic matter (e.g., humic acids) on the extraction yield was considered because of its presence in wastewater and soil [38]. As shown in Fig. 7 (n=3), a minor effect (∼5% reduction) on the extraction was observed at concentrations up to 0.2 mg L−1, which can be regarded within the limit of experimental error (see RSDs vide infra).

Also, the effect of the addition of organic solvent on the extraction was evaluated. The organic solvent was used for the extraction of organic matter from the soil, and then, this mixture was spiked to the extraction solution. Acetonitrile was added to the extraction solution at four different contents between 1% and 10% v/v. As shown in Fig. 8 , the effect of solvent on the extraction was negligible (∼4% reduction, (number of replicates n=3) for a content of organic solvent up to 5% v/v, which can be regarded as being within the limit of experimental error (see RSDs vide infra). Therefore, for the extraction of the organic compounds from the soil samples, 5% of acetonitrile was used.

The elution of the analytes was optimized by testing a variety of solvents from nonpolar to polar ones, i.e., n-hexane, dichloromethane, ethyl acetate, methanol, and acetonitrile. Maximum performance was exhibited using acetonitrile and ethyl acetate, with almost similar recoveries, expressed as the sum of the areas of the analytes. Maximum recovery of about 95% was accomplished by two successive extractions: the first one with 4 mL of acetonitrile and the second with 4 mL of ethyl acetate. Because acetonitrile is a more polar solvent than ethyl acetate, the first desorption was performed by acetonitrile to remove potential water residues. After that, the second desorption was performed with ethyl acetate to remove the rest of the organic compounds. Subsequently, another desorption cycle with 4 mL of acetonitrile and 4 mL of ethyl acetate was performed on the same sponge, to ascertain if the first elution cycle was adequate to achieve complete elution of analytes. The results in Fig. 9 (number of replicates n=3) showed that no analytes were retained on the sponge and thus, one elution cycle is sufficient.

Under the optimum conditions, the analytical characteristics of the proposed method for the quantitation of eleven PAHs and three musks were studied. Calibration plots were drawn for each analyte by analyzing DDW samples spiked with standard solutions of analytes, at concentrations of 0.015-11.8 µg L−1. At each concentration, three replicates were carried out. The linearity of the method was observed in the same range of concentrations and the coefficient of determination (R2) ranged from 0.9957 to 0.9992. The limits of detection (LOD, 3 x signal-to-noise ratio) and the limits of quantitation (LOQ, 10 x signal-to-noise ratio) were found to be in the range of 4.9-27 ng L−1and 15-80 ng L−1, respectively. The precision of the method, within-day and between three days, was calculated as the relative standard deviation (RSD) by analyzing three samples in each case, at concentrations of 0.8 µg L−1 and 2.6 µg L−1 for PAHs and musks, respectively. Within-day RSDs and inter-day RSDs for three replicates, ranged between 5.8 and 8.7% and 6.3 and 9.6%, respectively. A summary of the analytical merits is shown in Table 1 .

The applicability of the proposed method to the effluent and soil samples was evaluated by analyzing them, at two different concentrations (number of replicates n=3) and calculating the percentage of recovery of target analytes. The ranges of recoveries from the effluent sample were 83-106% and 89-102% at low (0.4 µg L−1 for PAHs and 0.3 µg L−1 for musks) and high concentrations (0.8 µg L−1 for PAHs and 0.6 µg L−1 for musks) respectively. The spiked extract of the soil sample was used as a matrix to assess the efficiency of the subsequent extraction/clean-up steps. The corresponding recoveries from soil samples were 70-107% and 79-112%, respectively. Omitting the sponge-based clean-up step in soil analysis, resulted in a number of extraneous peaks in the respective chromatogram and recoveries were unacceptably high for many of the analytes. Therefore, the presence of the modified sponge does not only extract the analytes but also contributes to the clean-up of the extracts. The above data are summarized in Table 1.

Table 2 compares the proposed method with other analytical methods for the determination of musks and PAHs in similar matrixes. The proposed method achieves high recoveries and satisfactory RSDs, which in many cases are better than those in previously reported methods. The main advantages of this analytical method, compared to other ones, is the simplicity of the adsorbent and its application to complex environmental matrixes. As shown in Table 2, previously reported analytical methods use complex adsorbents demanding cumbersome and time-consuming procedures for their synthesis, in contrast to MeS@GQDs, which requires only 3 h in an oven. Moreover, LODs are comparable or ever better than in most of the reported cases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and selectivity of the sorbent
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the reusability of the sorbent, already used MeS@GQDs were re-conditioned and the sponges were reused, following the optimized extraction procedure. The sponges, almost invariably, exhibited the same performance as in the first cycle of use, thus evidencing their ability for reuse. After the second cycle, it was noticed that part of GQDs started to detach from the sponge during the extraction process, resulting in a decrease in preconcentration capability. Moreover, the three-dimensional open hole structure of the sponge began to deteriorate to such a degree that it was visually perceptible, and the overall recovery was reduced by 30% compared with the first cycle of use.

To estimate the selectivity of MeS@GQDs to the tested analytes, the extraction efficiency of a number of pesticides was examined. The extraction was performed in spiked effluent and soil samples. The results showed that only five out of the twenty-seven examined pesticides were extracted from MeS@GQDs from the two samples. Two of them belong to the organophosphates (Fenthion, Bromophos methyl), the other two to the organochlorine category (Vinclozolin, alpha Endosulfan), and Tolyfluanid is a Sulphamide. The total extraction recovery of the five pesticides were 80.6% and 65.1% for the effluent and soil sample, respectively. Although the preference for the above pesticides is not explained by the hydrophobic and π interactions (neither all pesticides with increased LogK ow nor all with aromatic rings in their structure could be adsorbed), the developed sorbent material exhibited some selectivity over some commonly used pesticides, whereas we see prospect of it being used as a sorbent for some of them.

This study describes the functionalization of melamine sponge with GQDs. The new, functionalized melamine sponge holds great promise as a sorbent material capitalizing on the extractive properties of the GQDs. The as-synthesized hydrophobic MeS@GQDs was used to develop an alternative sample preparation procedure for the determination of PAHs and musks from environmental samples. The low cost of the materials needed for the preparation of MeS@GQDs (i.e., melamine sponge and citric acid) combined with the simplicity of the preparation, (requires only 3 h of heating), makes it ideal for the production on a larger scale. Moreover, the favorable analytical figures of merit and its potential for reuse, not only make the new sorbent material propitious for routine analyses, but also renders it a better alternative compared with other developed analytical methods.

Georgia Veneti: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Theodoros Chatzimitakos: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Constantine Stalikas: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.chroma.2022.463375.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found
